title: ## STANDARD FOR BRIE CXS 277-1973 Formerly CODEX STAN C-34-1973. Adopted in 1973. Revised in 2007. Amended in 2008, 2010, 2018, 2019, 2022.

sections.section: ## STANDARD FOR BRIE CXS 277-1973 Formerly CODEX STAN C-34-1973. Adopted in 1973. Revised in 2007. Amended in 2008, 2010, 2018, 2019, 2022.
sections.summary: Document heading identifying the Standard for Brie (CXS 277-1973), formerly CODEX STAN C-34-1973, with adoption and subsequent revision/amendment years listed (1973, revised 2007; amended 2008, 2010, 2018, 2019, 2022).

2022 Amendment: records a textual amendment replacing prior non-retail container labelling provisions with a requirement to follow the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
Scope: this standard applies to Brie intended for direct consumption or for further processing in accordance with the product description in Section 2.

Description: defines Brie as a soft, primarily white-mould surface-ripened cheese (flat cylinder or sectors) with a soft, non-crumbly body and white-mould rind that may show coloured spots; normal ripening for consumption is about 10 days at 10–16°C though alternative ripening methods yielding equivalent properties are permitted, and Brie intended for further processing may be less ripened.

Essential composition and quality factors: specifies permitted raw materials and ingredients (cow/buffalo milk and derived products; certain starter and other harmless cultures, rennet, salts, water, ripening enzymes, processing aids and limited anti-caking agents for cut/sliced products), sets required minimums for milkfat in dry matter and minimum dry matter levels by fat category, and states that compositional modifications beyond these limits are non-compliant with CXS 206-1999 Section 4.3.3.
Raw materials: only cows' milk, buffaloes' milk, their mixtures and products derived from these milks are permitted.
Essential sizes and shapes: specifies a maximum height of approximately 5 cm and a typical whole-cheese weight range of about 500 g to 3 500 g for flat-cylinder Brie.

Essential ripening procedure: rind formation and maturation (proteolysis) from surface to centre is predominantly caused by Penicillium candidium and/or Penicillium camembertii and Penicillium caseicolum.

Food additives: only additive classes justified in the standard's table may be used; colours are allowed in the cheese mass per the General Standard for Food Additives (CXS 192-1995) and only certain acidity regulators listed are acceptable for Brie.

Contaminants: products must comply with maximum contaminant and toxin levels in CXS 193-1995, and milk used must meet contaminant/toxin limits and CAC-established maximum residue limits for veterinary drugs and pesticides.

Hygiene: recommends preparing and handling Brie according to the General Principles of Food Hygiene (CXC 1-1969), the Code of Hygienic Practice for Milk and Milk Products (CXC 57-2004) and other relevant Codex texts, and complying with any microbiological criteria established per CXG 21-1997.
Section heading only: '7. LABELLING' is a header with no substantive labelling provisions included in this excerpt.

The name “Brie” may be used only if the cheese complies with this standard (alternative spellings customary in the retail country are allowed); if fat content falls outside the reference range but above the absolute minimum, the product name must be accompanied by a prominent qualification stating the modification or fat content (as % by mass, % fat in dry matter, or equivalent), and the designation may also be applied to cut, sliced, shredded or grated products made from compliant cheese.

The country of origin — defined as the country of manufacture, not the country where the name originated — must be declared, and if the product is substantially transformed in a second country that country is to be shown as the country of origin for labelling purposes.

Milk fat content must be declared in a manner acceptable in the country of retail sale, either as percentage by mass, percentage of fat in dry matter, or grams per serving (with the number of servings stated).
Labelling of non-retail containers should comply with the General Standard for the Labelling of Non-Retail Containers of Foods (CXS 346-2021).
Methods of analysis and sampling specified in the Recommended Methods of Analysis and Sampling (CXS 234-1999), relevant to this standard's provisions, shall be used to check compliance.
